title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identifying potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using HTC Vive headset with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', control) in a 4x4 randomized complete block design. Collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time from headset tracking) and subjective measures (feature ratings, receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire)
method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment, Randomized Complete Block Design
tasks: Walking across virtual crosswalk in front of FAV with external features
metrics: Feature ratings (7-point scale); Crossing time; Waiting time before crossing; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ); Personal Innovativeness scale; Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection only | No other traffic included in virtual environment to eliminate distractions | Features indicated only safe crossing conditions; vehicle stopped in every trial | First exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavior patterns | Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability to broader population
notes: VR platform validated in prior work (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); Walking speed in VR (1.54 m/sec) was faster than general walking speed (1.22 m/sec); Study approved by IRB at Mississippi State University; Participants compensated $20 for approximately 40-minute session

authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identifying potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior

claim_text: Pedestrians who frequently commit errors or exhibit aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly, even with external features

title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identifying potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using HTC Vive headset with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment, Randomized Complete Block Design

claim_text: Pedestrians who intentionally violate traffic rules or get distracted became more cautious in FAV presence and appreciated external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection only | No other traffic included in virtual environment to eliminate distractions | Features indicated only safe crossing conditions; vehicle stopped in every trial | First exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavior patterns | Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability to broader population

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, with the goal of identifying which pedestrian groups and intersection types are more likely to exhibit such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.
method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was developed using survey data collected from 631 pedestrians in Hefei, China. The questionnaire captured socioeconomic characteristics, trip-related features, and pedestrian attitudes toward crossing behavior. The Score test was used for variable selection at the 95% confidence level, followed by iterative calculation to identify significant predictors.
tasks: Pedestrian red-light running behavior prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples, May-June 2015)
metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Trip purpose significantly affects pedestrian red-light running behavior, with strolling pedestrians showing lower odds (OR=0.394) compared to those going to work or school
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings | Individual pedestrian behavior was analyzed without considering the impact of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Sample size, while sufficient for model performance, could be expanded across multiple intersections and cities for validation
notes: Study addresses pedestrian safety as both a traffic safety and public health issue in China; The model provides probability prediction function for red-light running behavior; Key insight: pedestrians affected by road facility quality are MORE likely to run red lights, possibly due to perceived lack of monitoring; The paper recommends countermeasures including traffic fines, education campaigns, improved road facilities, and variable speed limits

datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples, May-June 2015)
metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Time period in a day significantly affects red-light running probability, with daytime showing the highest odds (OR=3.326) compared to dawn
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings | Individual pedestrian behavior was analyzed without considering the impact of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Sample size, while sufficient for model performance, could be expanded across multiple intersections and cities for validation
notes: Study addresses pedestrian safety as both a traffic safety and public health issue in China; The model provides probability prediction function for red-light running behavior; Key insight: pedestrians affected by road facility quality are MORE likely to run red lights, possibly due to perceived lack of monitoring; The paper recommends countermeasures including traffic fines, education campaigns, improved road facilities, and variable speed limits

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, with the goal of identifying which pedestrian groups and intersection types are more likely to exhibit such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.

tasks: Pedestrian red-light running behavior prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples, May-June 2015)
metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians who indicate they will run a red-light when in hurry have significantly higher probability of such behavior (OR=0.299 for non-hurry group, indicating hurry increases risk)
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings | Individual pedestrian behavior was analyzed without considering the impact of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Sample size, while sufficient for model performance, could be expanded across multiple intersections and cities for validation
notes: Study addresses pedestrian safety as both a traffic safety and public health issue in China; The model provides probability prediction function for red-light running behavior; Key insight: pedestrians affected by road facility quality are MORE likely to run red lights, possibly due to perceived lack of monitoring; The paper recommends countermeasures including traffic fines, education campaigns, improved road facilities, and variable speed limits

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, with the goal of identifying which pedestrian groups and intersection types are more likely to exhibit such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.

tasks: Pedestrian red-light running behavior prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples, May-June 2015)
metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians' attitudes toward road facility quality significantly affect red-light running, with those believing facility quality affects crossing behavior more likely to run red lights (OR=0.483 for unaffected group)
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings | Individual pedestrian behavior was analyzed without considering the impact of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Sample size, while sufficient for model performance, could be expanded across multiple intersections and cities for validation
notes: Study addresses pedestrian safety as both a traffic safety and public health issue in China; The model provides probability prediction function for red-light running behavior; Key insight: pedestrians affected by road facility quality are MORE likely to run red lights, possibly due to perceived lack of monitoring; The paper recommends countermeasures including traffic fines, education campaigns, improved road facilities, and variable speed limits

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, with the goal of identifying which pedestrian groups and intersection types are more likely to exhibit such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.

The Score test was used for variable selection at the 95% confidence level, followed by iterative calculation to identify significant predictors.
tasks: Pedestrian red-light running behavior prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples, May-June 2015)
metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Four variables significantly predict red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period, attitude toward running when in hurry, and attitude toward road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings | Individual pedestrian behavior was analyzed without considering the impact of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Sample size, while sufficient for model performance, could be expanded across multiple intersections and cities for validation
notes: Study addresses pedestrian safety as both a traffic safety and public health issue in China; The model provides probability prediction function for red-light running behavior; Key insight: pedestrians affected by road facility quality are MORE likely to run red lights, possibly due to perceived lack of monitoring; The paper recommends countermeasures including traffic fines, education campaigns, improved road facilities, and variable speed limits
